title: Sticky Toffee Pudding
subtitle: Dates soaked in bicarbonate, a dark sponge, and toffee sauce poured twice
excerpt: A properly sticky sponge built on Medjool or standard dates dissolved in boiling water with bicarbonate of soda. The toffee sauce goes into the batter and over the finished pudding. Rich, dark, and served warm from the tin.
sourceNotes: Sticky toffee pudding was popularised by Francis Coulson at Sharrow Bay Hotel in the 1970s, though earlier similar recipes exist. The version here follows the proportions established in British restaurant cooking of that period: dates softened in bicarbonate water, a creamed sponge, and a butter-cream-sugar sauce applied before and after baking.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the sponge without the sauce for up to two months. Defrost at room temperature, then warm in the oven at 160°C fan and pour hot sauce over to serve.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the sponge a day ahead and keep covered at room temperature. Reheat portions in the microwave for 90 seconds and pour the warmed sauce over. The sauce keeps in the fridge for two weeks.

content.text: The dates are the whole point. Medjool dates give a deeper, jammy result than the small dried variety, but either works. The bicarbonate of soda isn't there to leaven the sponge in the conventional sense, it softens the date flesh and darkens the soaking liquid so it dissolves into the batter with almost no resistance. Leave out the bicarbonate and the dates stay in identifiable pieces; use it and the result is a uniformly dark, moist sponge throughout.

The sauce needs to go on twice: once over the baked sponge before returning it to the oven, and again in the bowl at the table. The first pour soaks in and turns the surface properly sticky. The second pour is what makes it restaurant-standard. Don't skip either.

items.prepNote: pitted and roughly chopped (standard dried dates also work)

content.text: A 30 × 20 cm baking tin or a deep roasting tin of similar dimensions. Grease and line the base with baking paper.

Put the chopped dates in a bowl and pour 250 ml of just-boiled water over them. Stir in {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} of bicarbonate of soda. Set aside for 10 minutes. The liquid will foam briefly; the dates will soften completely. Once cooled slightly, mash with a fork to a rough paste, leaving some texture is fine.

Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Beat {{unsalted-butter}} of softened butter with {{dark-brown-sugar}} of dark brown sugar until pale and slightly fluffy, about 3 minutes with a hand mixer. Add the eggs gradually, beating between additions. Stir in the vanilla. Fold in {{self-raising-flour}} of self-raising flour until just combined, then fold in the date mixture. The batter will be loose and dark.

Pour the batter into the prepared tin and spread level. Bake for 30, 35 minutes until a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean. The top should be set and springy; the sides will have pulled away slightly from the tin.

While the sponge bakes, combine {{double-cream}} of double cream, 150 g of dark brown sugar, and 50 g of butter in a medium saucepan. Bring to the boil over medium heat, stirring to dissolve the sugar, then simmer for 3 minutes until glossy. It will thicken more as it cools.
Soak and return to the oven

When the sponge comes out, pierce it all over with a skewer and pour half the warm sauce evenly over the top. Return to the oven for 5 minutes. The sauce will soak in and the surface will turn deeply glossy and sticky. Rest for 5 minutes before cutting. Serve warm with the remaining sauce poured at the table.

items.symptom: Sponge is dense and heavy rather than light
items.cause: The butter and sugar were not beaten long enough, or the eggs were added too fast and the batter split
items.fix: Beat the butter and sugar for a full 3 minutes before adding the eggs. Add eggs one at a time, or a tablespoon at a time, and beat well between additions.

items.symptom: Centre is still liquid after 35 minutes
items.cause: Oven runs cool, or the tin is too deep so the centre takes longer to cook through
items.fix: Test with a skewer, it should come out clean. Add 5-minute increments until done. Cover loosely with foil if the top is browning too fast.

items.cause: Sugar crystallised before dissolving, or sauce boiled too hard
items.fix: Melt the butter first, then add the cream and sugar together, and stir until dissolved before raising the heat. If grainy, add a splash of cream and heat gently, stirring.

items.symptom: Surface is not sticky after the second pour
items.cause: Sauce was too cool when poured, or not enough piercings were made
items.fix: Pierce generously, at least 20 holes in a 30 cm tin, and make sure the sauce is warm and pourable when it goes on. Return to the oven for the full 5 minutes.

content.text: Sticky toffee pudding arrived in British restaurant cooking in the 1970s, associated primarily with Sharrow Bay Hotel in the Lake District where Francis Coulson served it. The original used dates in a way that was then unusual in a hot pudding; the combination of bicarbonate-soaked dates and a double-application toffee sauce was novel enough to be widely copied. It has since become the canonical British restaurant dessert. The home version is no different in principle, and substantially better than any commercial frozen variant.
